2025 · arXiv · Agents

TRAIL: Trace Reasoning and Agentic Issue Localization

Can an LLM debug another agent? TRAIL hands frontier models 148 real, structured execution traces and 841 expert-annotated errors and asks them to say what went wrong and where. The best model — Gemini-2.5-Pro — scores just 11%, and three of eight models can't even fit the trace in context.

♪ listen — audio episode
3-voice deep dive · mp3 ↗ · rss feed ↗
Deshpande, Gangal, Mehta, Krishnan, Kannappan, Qian · Patronus AI
venue arXiv citations 47 topic Agents source ↗ listen ♪
// citation count as of 2026-06-19
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

An agent doesn't return an answer — it returns a trace: a long, interleaved sequence of model reasoning, tool calls, and tool outputs, often thousands of steps deep and logged across multiple sub-agents. When the agent fails, the interesting question isn't "did it solve the task?" (the end-to-end benchmark answer) but "which step broke, and why?" That is the question existing evaluation can't answer at scale.

Today the answer is a human reading the whole trace by hand — domain-specific, slow, and impossible to keep up with as agentic workflows multiply. And the obvious fix, "use an LLM to read the trace," runs into three walls the paper sets out to close. First, prior trace-analysis work parses traces into unstructured text, which doesn't match what real frameworks actually emit — standardized, structured OpenTelemetry spans — and LLMs are known to struggle with structured data. Second, the leading failure taxonomy (MAST) covers only reasoning and coordination, ignoring the execution-level failures that dominate production: API errors, rate limits, environment misconfiguration, resource exhaustion. Third, debugging an agent is genuinely harder than debugging software, because a failure can come from the model's reasoning or from a tool's output, and the two are tangled together. TRAIL argues you need a step-level, structured, real-world-grounded benchmark with a taxonomy granular enough to name execution and planning failures — not just a pass/fail score.

// how it works

The method

TRAIL has two parts: a formal error taxonomy and a dataset of 148 expert-annotated traces built against it. The taxonomy (Figure 1) sorts agentic failures into three areas. Reasoning Errors cover hallucinations (text-only and tool-related), information processing (poor retrieval, tool-output misinterpretation), decision making (incorrect problem identification, tool-selection errors), and output generation (formatting errors, instruction non-compliance). System Execution Errors cover configuration (bad tool definitions, environment setup), API/system faults (rate limiting 429, auth 401/403, service 500, resource-not-found 404), and resource management (exhaustion, timeouts). Planning & Coordination Errors cover context management (context-handling failures, resource abuse) and task management (goal deviation, task-orchestration errors). The execution and planning branches are the part prior taxonomies leave out.

The traces are grounded in two hard, widely-used agent benchmarks. For open-world search, TRAIL runs the Hugging Face OpenDeepResearch hierarchical multi-agent system (a manager agent plus a search agent with web tools) on GAIA, backed by o3-mini. For software engineering, it runs a single-agent CodeAct loop with a sandboxed Python interpreter and the gitingest library on SWE-Bench Lite, backed by claude-3-7-sonnet. Errors are induced organically — the agents are given realistic constraints (output-length limits, forced exploration) that provoke natural failures rather than scripted ones. Every run is logged through OpenTelemetry's openinference standard, so each trace is a real sequence of structured spans (Figure 2), not parsed prose.

Four annotators with software-engineering and log-debugging expertise labeled the traces; a separate 63-trace set measured inter-annotator agreement (reported high), at $12.66 per trace and 30–40 minutes each. The result is 148 traces, 1,987 spans (575 of them carrying at least one error), and 841 annotated errors — an average of 5.68 errors per trace. The evaluation task is then deliberately simple to state and hard to do: give a model the full structured trace and have it act as a judge, predicting for every error both its category and its location (which span). Scoring is per-trace Category F1 and Location Accuracy, with the headline Joint Accuracy counting a prediction only when both are right.

Joint = # errors with correct category AND correct span# annotated errors // naming the failure isn't enough — the judge must also pin the exact span it occurred in
// what they found

The results

Trace debugging is mostly unsolved. The best model, Gemini-2.5-Pro, reaches only 11% combined Joint Accuracy across both splits — 18% on GAIA and 5% on SWE-Bench. Every other model is worse, and the task is brutal on context: three of eight models (o1, o3, Claude-3.7-Sonnet) cannot even fit the SWE-Bench traces in their context window (marked CLE below). The traces are genuinely enormous — the longest GAIA input is 7.5M tokens against a 1M-token limit, and the maximum trace length is always about twice the model's input limit.

Model (GAIA split)Cat. F1Loc. Acc.Joint
Gemini-2.5-Pro *0.3890.5460.183
Gemini-2.5-Flash *0.3370.3720.100
OpenAI o3 *0.2960.5350.092
Claude-3.7-Sonnet *0.2540.2040.047
GPT-4.10.2180.1070.028
OpenAI o1 *0.1380.0400.013
Llama-4-Maverick0.1220.0230.000
Llama-4-Scout0.0410.0000.000

// * = reasoning effort set to "high". Averages of three runs.

Two structural findings stand out. Reasoning helps. Every reasoning model except o1 beats the non-reasoning models on both Category F1 and Location Accuracy, and on Joint Accuracy the reasoning family runs 1.5–8× the best non-reasoning score. Pushing the same model harder helps too: dialing o3 from "high" to "low" reasoning effort drops Category F1 monotonically (0.296 → 0.277 → 0.264). Length hurts. All performance metrics are anti-correlated with input length — Location Accuracy at Pearson r = −0.379 — so a real part of the difficulty is simply fitting and tracking a giant trace.

The error distribution itself is lopsided (Figure 3). Output Generation dominates: Formatting Errors and Instruction Non-compliance alone account for 353 of 841 errors (~42%), while whole System Execution categories — API failures, auth, resource limits — sit in the long tail with a handful of examples each. That tail matters precisely because those rare errors are often catastrophic and unrecoverable, which is the paper's argument for a taxonomy granular enough to name them.

Breaking F1 down per category (Figure 4) shows models are uneven, not uniformly weak. Some columns are near-zero for almost everyone — Context Handling Failures sits at ≈0.00 for all but Claude-3.7 (0.18). Some are cracked by a single model — Tool Selection Errors reach 0.53 only for o3, and Task Orchestration only for Gemini-2.5-Flash (0.47). Goal Deviation peaks with Gemini-2.5-Pro at 0.70. Hallucination-style "Language-Only" errors are caught reasonably well by everyone (0.14–0.59), while Formatting is oddly non-monotonic: older, non-reasoning GPT-4.1 (0.43) outscores o3 and Claude-3.7 (0.23–0.31) on it.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The headline — "frontier LLMs score 11% at trace debugging" — is real and useful, but it bundles together two things that the benchmark can't cleanly separate: reasoning skill and raw context capacity. With the longest traces running 2× the input limit and three of eight models literally unable to read the SWE-Bench inputs, a meaningful share of the low scores is "the model couldn't fit the trace," not "the model read it and reasoned badly." The paper is honest about this — it reports the input-length anti-correlation directly — but the framing still invites reading a long-context stress test as a pure reasoning verdict.

baselines

The "baselines" are a fair, current 2025 judge pool (o3, Claude-3.7, Gemini-2.5, GPT-4.1, Llama-4), all evaluated zero-shot. But there's no fine-tuned trace-debugger baseline and — crucially — no human ceiling on the judging task: we know annotators agree with each other, but not what Joint Accuracy a human reaches, so "11%" has no top reference. The trace-generating backbones (o3-mini, Claude-3.7) also overlap with the judge pool.

contamination

Low risk on the right axis: traces are freshly generated, so the debugging targets aren't in pretraining. The underlying GAIA / SWE-Bench-Lite tasks are public, but that shouldn't help localize errors. The bigger confound isn't leakage — it's that scores entangle context length with reasoning.

what's missed

Errors are induced via prompt constraints (output-length caps, forced exploration), which plausibly inflates the Output-Generation share (~42%) relative to how organic agents fail. The category distribution is severely imbalanced, so per-category F1 on the rare-but-catastrophic execution errors rests on a few examples each and is high-variance. The SWE split is only ~30 error-bearing traces, so its Joint = 0.05 is a small-sample number.

would raise confidence

A human Joint-Accuracy ceiling on the same task; an ablation holding context length fixed (truncated or retrieval-fed traces) to split long-context capacity from reasoning; more than one scaffold/backbone per domain; a larger SWE split; and variance/CIs on the 3-run averages and the tail-category F1s.

// bottom line

TRAIL convincingly establishes that frontier LLMs can't yet act as automated trace debuggers, and its structured-OpenTelemetry, execution-and-planning-aware taxonomy is a genuine step past reasoning-only failure taxonomies. Just read the 11% as a joint verdict on reasoning and context capacity under an induced-error distribution — not as a clean measurement of trace-reasoning alone.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Trace debugging is an open problem. The best frontier judge, Gemini-2.5-Pro, reaches only 11% joint accuracy at classifying and locating errors, and 3 of 8 models can't even fit the trace in context.
  2. The contribution is structure plus taxonomy. 148 real OpenTelemetry traces and 841 errors, grounded in GAIA and SWE-Bench Lite, organized by a three-area taxonomy (reasoning / system execution / planning) that finally names execution-level failures like rate limits and resource exhaustion.
  3. It's partly a long-context benchmark. Performance anti-correlates with input length and the longest traces run 2× the context window — so closing the gap needs both better reasoning and cheaper, larger context, not one or the other.
